---
title: "Naive Bayes Classifier"
format: html
---

## Overview

Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier based on Bayes' theorem with an independence assumption between features. Despite this simplifying assumption, it often performs well in practice, trains very quickly, and produces well-calibrated probability estimates.

## Parameters

| Parameter | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Epsilon | 1e-9 | Variance smoothing — added to feature variances to prevent division by zero |

## When to Use

- You need fast training (near-instant, even with many features)
- Your features are roughly independent of each other
- You want probability estimates for each class
- You have a multi-class problem with many classes

## Probability Output

Naive Bayes produces probability estimates derived directly from Bayes' theorem. The probabilities across all classes sum to 1.0 for each frame.

## Tips

- Naive Bayes works best when features are not strongly correlated
- If accuracy is poor, consider whether features are redundant (correlated) — try reducing to independent features
- The epsilon parameter rarely needs adjustment unless you have features with extremely low variance
